original_hypothesis.title: ERC20Vault changeBridgedToken leaves stale bridgedToCanonical mapping for old token, enabling future logic confusion or bypass in upgrades/tools
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken attempts to delete a mapping entry but deletes bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew] instead of bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]. Since _btokenNew was required to be unmapped, this deletion is a no-op and the old bridged token remains mapped to the canonical token forever while also being blacklisted. This breaks the canonical<->bridged bijection invariant and can cause future contract upgrades, off-chain indexers, or emergency tools that rely on bridgedToCanonical alone (and forget to check blacklist) to treat the old token as still valid for burn/mint accounting.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Stale state is a frequent root cause of operational incidents during migrations/upgrades, particularly when multiple systems (indexers, risk engines, bridges on other chains) assume one-to-one mappings.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]; btokenBlacklist[btokenOld_]=true; bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] not deleted

preconditions: At least one token migration performed via changeBridgedToken
A later code path/tool uses bridgedToCanonical without checking btokenBlacklist

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Migration produces inconsistent on-chain registry state across two mappings

operational_questions: Do any in-scope contracts or off-chain services rely on bridgedToCanonical for validity without checking btokenBlacklist?
Are there admin rescue/migration scripts that could be confused by stale mappings?
Do upgrades plan to iterate over mappings or assume bijection?
validation_requirements: Write a migration test: migrate token, confirm bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] persists
Review all usages of bridgedToCanonical and confirm blacklist is always enforced
Assess indexer and monitoring assumptions around token registry

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for a token migration to occur
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Target any component that trusts bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] as authoritative
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Use old token in that component to induce incorrect mint/burn/valuation actions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Depends on existence of a relying component; may require upgrade timing

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Mis-accounting, stuck migrations, erroneous UI/indexer state
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium; requires upgrade or explicit cleanup migration
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Token migration operations and token registry integrity

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: BridgedTokenChanged events followed by lingering bridgedToCanonical for old token
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Off-chain services showing two bridged tokens for one canonical token
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users attempting to use blacklisted btoken in unexpected flows

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
